title: The One Where Eddie Moves In

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Huh? So whaddya think? Casa de Joey. Huh? I decorated it myself.
Ross: Get out.
All: No.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 말도 안 돼! / 설마! — 상대방의 말에 놀라거나 믿기 어려울 때 나오는 감탄사. 물리적으로 나가라는 뜻이 아님.
expressions.nuance: "No way!" 또는 "You're kidding!"과 같은 뉘앙스로, 놀람과 불신을 동시에 표현한다. 지금도 일상 구어에서 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "I won the lottery." "Get out! Are you serious?" — "나 복권 당첨됐어." "말도 안 돼! 진짜야?"

occ.speaker: Marsha
occ.text: He's out banging other women over the head with a club, while she sits at home trying to get the mastodon smell out of the carpet!

occ.speaker: Woman
occ.text: Yeah, well, I had a 24-inch waist. You lose things. Now come on, get outta my way.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Oh.. you don't get it! (Passes out and slumps across her)

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Wow Joey, this is, uhh...
Joey: Art.
Monica: Art it is.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 그래, [명사]이네. — 상대방이 정의한 것을 온전히 납득하지 못하더라도 건조하게 수용하며 동의하는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 아이러니나 유머가 담길 때가 많다. 상대방의 주장을 그대로 돌려주는 패턴으로, 지금도 쓰인다. 한국어 '그래, 그거로 하자'와 달리 약간의 체념이나 냉소가 섞이는 경우가 많다.
expressions.example: "Let's call it a 'creative disagreement.'" "Creative disagreement it is." — "그냥 '창의적 의견 충돌'이라고 하자." "그래, 창의적 의견 충돌이네."

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Yeah well, I hear the place looks great.
Joey: Ahh, forget about it, I'm havin' a ball.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 신나게 즐기다, 대단히 즐거운 시간을 보내다.
expressions.nuance: 파티, 여행, 새로운 생활 등 즐거운 경험을 묘사할 때 쓴다. 여전히 쓰이지만 약간 클래식한 느낌이 있어 어른스럽거나 레트로한 뉘앙스를 줄 수 있다.
expressions.example: "How was the weekend trip?" "We had a ball — highly recommend that place." — "주말 여행 어땠어?" "완전 신났어, 강력 추천해."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Monica, you don't even have a bed, you sleep in a ball on the floor!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Hey, if they have a ball maybe you can stick razor blades in it and teach them a new game, Gonna Need Stitches Ball.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Well, we're not here to meet guys.  You have a boyfriend, I have a b. . . baby and a Ross.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Yeah well, I hear the place looks great.
Joey: Ahh, forget about it, I'm havin' a ball.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 이 맥락에서는 '말할 것도 없지, 더 말할 필요도 없이 (좋아)'의 뜻. 하지만 문맥에 따라 '그건 신경 꺼', '말도 안 돼(감탄)', '물론이지' 등 의미가 완전히 달라진다.
expressions.nuance: 뉴욕·이탈리아계 미국인 슬랭으로, 글자 그대로 '잊어버려'가 아닌 경우가 훨씬 많다. 감탄할 때, 제안을 거절할 때, 상황을 일축할 때 각각 다르게 쓰이는 것이 핵심.
expressions.example: "Was the pasta good?" "Forget about it — best I've ever had." — "파스타 맛있었어?" "말할 것도 없지, 내 인생 최고였어."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (as Ursula): Yeah, um... (nervously clears her throat) You know you, you should just forget about what I said under the bridge, I was talkin' crazy that night, I was so drunk!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Forget about it.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, you're right I, I should play in the hay. Forget about the fact that I just dropped 400 dollars to replace a bracelet that I hated to begin with. Bring on the hay. [sits down at the bar]

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Well uh, it's cause he had a thing with, wi-, with the thing.
Joey: Right, I go-, I got it.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 알겠어, 이해했어 — 굳이 끝까지 설명하지 않아도 맥락을 파악했다는 뜻.
expressions.nuance: 상대방이 말을 다 끝내기도 전에 이미 알아들었다는 신호로 쓴다. 때로는 '알았다고, 그만해도 돼'처럼 약간 퉁명스럽게 느껴질 수도 있다.
expressions.example: "You don't need to use too much, just a little—" "I got it, I got it." — "너무 많이 쓸 필요 없어, 조금만—" "알겠다고, 알겠어."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Great story!  But, I uh, I gotta go, I got a date with Andrea--Angela--Andrea...  Oh man, (looks to Chandler)

occ.speaker: Alan
occ.text: Yeah, I'm sorry too. But, I gotta tell you, I am a little relieved.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Alright. Once I got on the subway, right, and it was at night, and I rode it all the way to Brooklyn... just for the hell of it.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: Anyway, OK, now promise you won't like, freak out and say how great this is until I'm done, OK.
All: OK.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 흥분하다, 과잉반응하다 — 충격적이거나 흥분되는 상황에서 감정을 폭발시키는 것. 긍정적·부정적 상황 모두 가능.
expressions.nuance: 한국어 '난리 치다'와 가장 가까운 표현. 좋은 소식에 흥분하는 것도, 나쁜 소식에 패닉하는 것도 모두 'freak out'이다. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "Don't freak out, but I accidentally deleted the file." — "흥분하지 말고, 근데 실수로 파일 삭제했어."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Uh-huh. Okay. Okay, don't you think, maybe, though, it's just that he's so perceptive that it freaks you out?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Dad, before I was born, did you freak out at all?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Oh, I tasted Ben's milk, and Ross freaked out.

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: [singing] Smelly cat, smell-ly cat, what are they feeding you?
Producer: Alrighty. From the top.
Phoebe: OK. [singing] Smelly cat, smell-ly cat...
expressions.speaker: Producer
expressions.meaning: 처음부터 다시 시작하자 — 음악이나 공연에서 맨 처음 마디부터 다시 하자는 뜻.
expressions.nuance: 원래 음악·공연 용어이지만 일반 대화에서도 '처음부터 다시 해보자'는 의미로 확장되어 쓰인다. 'from the top' 단독으로 쓰거나 'let's take it from the top' 형태로도 쓴다.
expressions.example: "I got confused halfway through the explanation. Can we take it from the top?" — "설명 중간에 헷갈렸어. 처음부터 다시 해줄 수 있어?"

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: I wanna watch Entertainment Tonight.
Ross: Tough noogies, we're watching Predators of the Serengetti.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 안됐지만 어쩔 수 없어, 그건 네 문제야 — 'Tough luck'의 유치하고 장난스러운 버전.
expressions.nuance: 어린아이처럼 구는 상황이나 형제자매 간의 말다툼에서 나오는 표현. 'Tough luck'이나 'Too bad'보다 훨씬 더 유치한 뉘앙스라, 의도적으로 어린아이처럼 굴 때 씀.
expressions.example: "I wanted the last slice of pizza." "Tough noogies — I got here first." — "피자 마지막 조각 내가 원했는데." "안됐지만 내가 먼저 왔어."

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: [childish voice] Mi-mi-mii.
Rachel: [sarcastically] I've never wanted you more.
...
Rachel: Now I'm mommy in this little play? Alright look, I refuse to get sucked into this like, weird little Geller dimension thing OK.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 원하지 않는데도 어떤 상황에 끌려 들어가다, 휘말리다.
expressions.nuance: 진공청소기에 빨려들 듯 상황에 빠져드는 이미지의 표현. 드라마, 갈등, 논쟁 등 부정적인 상황에서 주로 쓰인다. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "I swore I wouldn't get sucked into another argument, and yet here I am." — "다신 그런 싸움에 휘말리지 않겠다고 했는데, 결국 이꼴이 됐네."

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Pretty cool, huh? But if you're thinking you can put a fish in there and it wouldn't get sucked up into the mechanism, well you'd be wrong.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Why did you hate me?
Monica: Because, you were mean to me... I mean I, I, loved you in a 'you're my brother so I have to' kind of way, but basically, yeah, I hated your guts.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: ~한 식으로, ~한 방식으로 — 감정이나 상황을 정확히 표현하기 어려울 때 그 성질을 부드럽게 수식하는 완충 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'in a [표현] kind of way'는 직접 말하기 애매하거나 완충이 필요할 때 쓰는 패턴. 한국어로는 '그런 식의'나 '그런 의미에서'에 해당하며, 일상 구어에서 매우 자주 등장한다.
expressions.example: "I miss the old job, in a 'it was familiar' kind of way." — "옛날 직장이 그립긴 해, 익숙했다는 그런 의미에서."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (Dancing in a swirly, Phoebe kind of way) I'm totally getting it!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I'm sorry it's just, Phoebe just always thought you were, you were charming in a, in a sexless kind of way.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's the big deal with her? Maybe she's attractive in an obvious kind of way.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: Look, Chandler, he has moved on, OK, you have to too.
Ross: You're just gonna have to accept the fact that you're just friends now, OK.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 어쩔 수 없이 ~해야 해, 그냥 ~할 수밖에 없어 — 달갑지 않은 현실을 받아들이거나 어떤 행동을 해야 한다는 것을 강조.
expressions.nuance: 'gonna have to'는 'going to have to'의 구어 축약. 상대방에게 힘든 현실을 받아들이라고 부드럽지만 단호하게 말할 때 자주 쓰인다. 한국어의 '~할 수밖에'와 뉘앙스가 유사하다.
expressions.example: "You're just gonna have to be patient — results take time." — "그냥 기다릴 수밖에 없어, 결과가 나오려면 시간이 걸려."

occ.speaker: Carol
occ.text: What that woman... did. I am not doin' that. It's just gonna have to stay in, that's all, everything will be the same, it'll just stay in.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Look honey, I wanted you to hook up with Ross as much as you did. But he's with her now and you're just gonna have to get over it.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: That's good, have a seat. Um, the doctor says it's gotta be a needle. You're just gonna have to be brave, ok? Can you do that for me?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Yeah. You're just gonna have to stop pissing me off.
Ross: I can do that.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 누군가를 짜증나게 하다, 열받게 하다.
expressions.nuance: 'annoy'보다 강도가 높은 구어 표현이지만, 일상 대화에서 매우 흔하게 쓰인다. 친한 사이에서는 유머처럼 쓰일 수도 있다. 지금도 널리 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "Stop pissing me off with your last-minute changes." — "막판에 자꾸 바꿔서 나를 열받게 하지 마."

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, he's in the back. The duck pissed him off, said that eggs came first.

occ.speaker: Joey groans and gets off the phone
occ.text: The producer from Days left a message on my machine asking why I wasn't at the parade. They said everybody's pissed off at me.. <whiny voice> And they all got to meet Santa!

occ.speaker: Wendy
occ.text: Besides, I can't leave until their Christmas party downstairs clears out; there are some *pissed off* insurance people looking for that ham.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: You know it's funny you should mention that 'cause I was thinkin'... what's with the boxes?
Chandler: Oh, uhh, actually I uh, have some news.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 왜 이게 있어? / 이게 무슨 상황이야? — 뭔가 평소와 다르거나 이상한 것을 발견했을 때 묻는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'What's with + 명사'는 설명이 필요한 상황이나 사람의 태도를 캐주얼하게 물을 때 쓰는 패턴. 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "What's with the long face? Did something happen?" — "왜 그렇게 우울한 표정이야? 무슨 일 있어?"

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: See ya.... Waitwait, what's with you?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (trying desperately to change the subject) So, Ross, what's going on with you? Any stories? (Digs her elbow into his hand.) No news, no little anecdotes to share with the folks?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on with you?

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Oh, now it's a spare room?
Chandler: Well yeah, in that it's not being used and I... have it to spare.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: ~라는 의미에서, ~하다는 점에서 — 자신의 주장이나 표현을 구체적으로 부연 설명하거나 근거를 댈 때 쓰는 접속 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'in the sense that' 또는 'because'와 유사하게 쓰인다. 약간 격식체 느낌이 있지만 구어에서도 쓰이며, 한국어 '~라는 의미에서'에 해당한다.
expressions.example: "It was a good day, in that nothing went wrong." — "아무 일도 없었다는 점에서는 좋은 하루였어."

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Sometimes I wish I was a lesbian... (They all stare at him.) Did I say that out loud?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Everything you need to know is in that first kiss.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I know. I just wish that once, I'd bring a guy home that they actually liked.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: So how you two gettin' along?
Chandler: Oh, I couldn't be happier.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 이보다 더 행복할 수 없어 — 더할 나위 없이 기쁘고 만족스럽다는 최상급 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'couldn't be + 비교급'은 '이보다 더 ~할 수 없다'는 뜻의 구어 패턴. 'I couldn't be more excited / proud / pleased' 등으로 응용 가능하며, 지금도 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "We couldn't be happier with how the event turned out." — "행사가 이렇게 잘 된 것에 이보다 더 기쁠 수가 없어."

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: See, this is the brilliance of the show. I say always keep them running. All the time, running. Run. Run Yasmine, run like the wind.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 바람처럼 달리다 — 매우 빠르게 달리다.
expressions.nuance: 과장된 속도를 표현하는 관용구. 유머러스하거나 과장된 맥락에서 자주 쓰이며, 실제로도 빠른 움직임을 묘사할 때 쓴다. 지금도 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "When the dog got off the leash, it ran like the wind and disappeared around the corner." — "개가 줄에서 풀리자 바람처럼 달려 모퉁이 너머로 사라졌어."

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: Well, OK, the record company sent over this piece of paper for me to sign, saying that it's OK for someone else to sing for me. That was my first clue.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 그게 첫 번째 단서였어 — 당연히 눈치챘어야 했는데 이제야 깨달았다는 아이러니컬한 표현.
expressions.nuance: '(Gee,) that was my first clue'처럼 반어적으로 쓰여, '그 뻔한 신호를 이제야 알아챘다'는 자조적 뉘앙스를 준다. 지금도 쓰이며, 자책이나 유머의 맥락에서 자주 등장한다.
expressions.example: "He canceled three times in a row. That was my first clue he wasn't interested." — "그가 세 번 연속 약속을 취소했어. 그게 관심 없다는 첫 번째 단서였어."

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Naa, you're just sayin' that 'cause you're in love with Yasmine Blepe.
Chandler: How could anyone not be in love with Yasmine Blepe?
Chandler: See, this is the brilliance of the show. I say always keep them running.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 그게 바로 [X의] 묘미야 / 핵심이야 — 어떤 것의 가장 독창적이거나 매력적인 부분을 강조할 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 상대방이 이해하지 못하는 매력 포인트를 설명할 때, 또는 역설적이고 단순한 것의 강점을 부각할 때 자주 쓰인다. 유머러스하거나 약간 과장된 맥락에서도 잘 어울린다.
expressions.example: "The app looks almost too simple." "That's the brilliance of it — anyone can use it." — "그 앱이 너무 단순해 보여." "그게 바로 묘미야, 누구나 쓸 수 있잖아."

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Okay, everybody relax. This is not even a date. It's just two people going out to dinner and- not having sex.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, I think this is the episode of Three's Company where there's some kind of misunderstanding.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Um, not so good. He walked me to the subway and said 'We should do this again!'

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: OK, Phoebs. But what about you?
Phoebe: Well I have a video, you have to pay attention. No, this voice woman, she's so talented but, according to the producer people, they said she doesn't have like the right look or something.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 외모가 어떤 역할이나 자리에 딱 맞다 / 적합한 이미지를 갖추다.
expressions.nuance: 연예계, 광고, 미디어 등에서 외모 적합성을 이야기할 때 쓰는 표현. 'look'을 명사로 써서 외모·이미지를 나타내는 구어적 용법이며, 지금도 업계 용어로 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "She has the talent, but the casting director said she doesn't have the right look for the role." — "실력은 있는데 캐스팅 디렉터가 그 역할에 외모가 맞지 않는다고 했어."

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: And he's happy, he's, he's decorated.
Rachel: Look, Chandler, he has moved on, OK, you have to too.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 앞으로 나아가다, 극복하고 다음 단계로 넘어가다 — 이별, 실직, 우정의 변화 등 상실 상황을 뒤로하고 새로운 시작을 하다.
expressions.nuance: 한국어 '잊어버려'보다 능동적으로 앞으로 나아간다는 뉘앙스가 강하다. 이별뿐 아니라 직업, 생각, 습관 등 다양한 상황에 쓰이며 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "It's been a year since the breakup. It's time to move on." — "헤어진 지 1년이 됐어. 이제 앞으로 나아갈 때야."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Well, he made a move on me.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: He's right, man. Please. Move on. Go to China. Eat Chinese food.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: I know, I know I was, but there was always this little voice inside that kept saying it's never gonna happen, move on. You know whose voice that was?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: I can't believe he has a new roommate. Who is this guy?
Ross: Uh, Eddie something. He just met him.
Rachel: It'll never last, he's just a rebound roommate.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 이별(또는 다른 상실) 후 공백을 채우기 위해 급하게 선택한 [명사].
expressions.nuance: 원래 이별 후 급하게 만나는 'rebound relationship'에서 온 개념. Rachel이 창의적으로 'rebound roommate'에 적용했는데, 이처럼 'rebound + 명사' 패턴을 다양한 맥락에 응용할 수 있다.
expressions.example: "He took the first job offer after quitting — classic rebound career move." — "그 사람 그만두자마자 들어온 첫 번째 제안 바로 수락했어, 전형적인 충동적 커리어 선택이지."

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Well, he doesn't really love her. I mean, it's just a rebound thing from me.... You'll see!

occ.speaker: Michelle
occ.text: No don't worry, this is not some rebound thing. I am totally over Eric.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: It's my TV.
Ross: Wha-, oh, quit it.
Monica: Bite me.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 꺼져, 웃기지 마, 닥쳐 — 상대방의 말을 강하게 거부하거나 무시할 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 강하고 무례한 표현이지만, 친한 사이나 형제자매 사이에서는 장난스럽게 쓰이기도 한다. 'shut up'보다 약간 더 공격적인 뉘앙스. 지금도 쓰인다.
expressions.example: A: "You're going to mess this up like always." B: "Oh, bite me." — A: "넌 또 망칠 거야." B: "웃기지 마."

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (singing/screaming) You're all invited to bite me!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Hmm, bite me.

occ.speaker: Rick
occ.text: Ow! Did you just bite me?

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Well, Monica keeps changin' the channel.
Monica: Oh that's great, why don't you tell mommy on me.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 누군가를 고자질하다, 이르다.
expressions.nuance: 원래 어린아이들의 행동을 묘사하는 표현이지만, 어른들이 유치하게 구는 상황을 빗댈 때도 쓰인다. 여기서 Monica는 Ross가 어린아이처럼 군다는 걸 빈정대며 쓰고 있다.
expressions.example: "Go ahead and tell the manager on me if you want." — "원하면 어디 가서 매니저한테 이르든지 해."

occ.speaker: Mr. Geller
occ.text: I'm not gonna tell you what they spent on that wedding... but forty thousand dollars is a lot of money!

occ.speaker: Aurora
occ.text: ...Luckily none of the bullets hit the engine block. So, we made it to the border, but just barely, and I- ...I've been talking about myself all night long, I'm sorry. What about you? Tell me one of your stories.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Oh man. Please tell me one of 'em is Ma.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: Hey, how come, uhh, Chandler didn't come?
Ross: Well uh, it's cause he had a thing with, wi-, with the thing.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 왜?, 어째서? — 'why'의 구어적 대안.
expressions.nuance: 'Why'보다 더 캐주얼하고 구어적이다. 핵심 문법 포인트: 'how come + 주어 + 동사' 순으로 의문문 도치를 쓰지 않는 것이 특징 ('How come he didn't come?' O, 'How come didn't he come?' X). 지금도 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "How come you didn't mention this earlier?" — "왜 이걸 좀 더 일찍 말 안 했어?"

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Ohh-ho-hooohhh. The hair comes out, and the gloves come on.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (in baby-like voice) How come you don't live with Mommy? (pause; shows Ross less than amused) How come Mommy lives with that other lady? (pause; Ross still looks less than amused; Joey smiling) What's a lesbian? (playfully hits Ross)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: No, no... how come you are working here?

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Listen, I'm, I'm sorry I didn't make it over there today.
Joey: Oh, that's OK. You uh, you had a thing.
Chandler: Yeah well, I hear the place looks great.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: ~라고 들었어, ~라더라 — 간접적으로 얻은 정보를 캐주얼하게 전달할 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.nuance: 'I hear'는 'I've heard'와 같은 의미. 소문이나 간접 정보를 부드럽고 가볍게 꺼낼 때 쓴다. 직접 확인한 게 아니라 전해 들었다는 뉘앙스를 자연스럽게 담을 수 있다.
expressions.example: "I hear you've been promoted. Congratulations!" — "승진했다고 들었어. 축하해!"

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (as Rachel is cutting up her cards) Y'know, if you listen closely, you can hear a thousand retailers scream.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Okay. Okay, let's say I bought a really great pair of shoes. Do you know what I'd hear, with every step I took? 'Not-mine. Not-mine. Not-mine.' And even if I was happy, okay, and, and skipping- 'Not-not-mine, not-not-mine, not-not-mine, not-not-mine'...

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Little louder, okay, I think there's a man on the twelfth floor in a coma that didn't quite hear you...
